releases.headline: Hybrid ECIES completes the PQC stack
releases.summary: API key exchange — the last classical-only component — is replaced with a hybrid ML-KEM-768 + ECDH P-384 construction. No plaintext key material appears in any HTTP response and AES-GCM is no longer used anywhere in the server.

items.title: API key exchange upgraded to hybrid ECIES
items.body: The wire protocol now performs ML-KEM-768 encapsulation alongside ECDH P-384, combines both shared secrets through HKDF-SHA3-256 into a wrapping key, and encrypts the per-session key with XChaCha20-Poly1305. The previous AES-GCM + SHA-256 + P-256 path is gone.

items.title: AES-GCM removed from the server
items.body: With the ECIES upgrade, no code path on the server still references AES-GCM. Every symmetric operation uses XChaCha20-Poly1305 and every hash uses a SHA-3 family function.

items.title: `lib/vendor/noble-pq.cjs` — ML-KEM-768 CommonJS bundle
items.body: A server-side CommonJS bundle of the noble post-quantum library provides ML-KEM-768 for the ECIES path. The bundle is vendored under `lib/vendor/` and carries no runtime npm dependency.

items.title: No plaintext key material in HTTP responses
items.body: Session keys are only ever transmitted as XChaCha20-Poly1305 ciphertext under the hybrid wrapping key. A passive observer who records the full HTTP exchange cannot recover the session key without breaking both ML-KEM-768 and ECDH P-384.

releases.headline: PQC on outbound connections and mutual TLS for sync clients
releases.summary: Every outbound HTTPS connection now negotiates PQC hybrid groups, and the server doubles as a small ECDSA P-384 CA that issues client certificates for mutual TLS on the sync channel.

items.title: Global PQC HTTPS agent for all outbound traffic
items.body: S3, SMTP, the Resend API, webhook deliveries, and Google OAuth all share a single outbound agent that advertises PQC hybrid groups. Operators do not need to configure PQC per integration; it is applied uniformly to every outbound HTTPS call.

items.title: `PQC_OUTBOUND_ENFORCE=false` for classical fallback
items.body: Set this environment variable to allow outbound classical handshakes during a transition window where an upstream service does not yet speak a PQC group. The default is enforcement on.

items.title: Built-in Certificate Authority for mTLS client certs
items.body: On first boot the server generates an ECDSA P-384 CA via OpenSSL and persists the private key with `0600` permissions. The CA issues short-lived client certificates whose SHA3-512 fingerprint is recorded for revocation.

items.title: One-click client certificate bundle download
items.body: Minting a sync token now also issues a client certificate; the admin UI returns a single PEM bundle (key + cert + CA) that the operator pastes into the desktop sync client.

items.title: Certificate revocation table with hashed fingerprints
items.body: Revoked certificates are tracked by SHA3-512 fingerprint hash. The mTLS validator consults the table on every handshake; a revoked client cert is refused before the WebSocket upgrade is attempted.

items.title: `MTLS_REQUIRED=true` to enforce client certificates on sync
items.body: When set, every sync WebSocket upgrade must present a valid client certificate in addition to the API key (dual auth). When unset, mTLS is offered but not required, easing rollout to existing clients.

items.title: Temporary certificate files cleaned up immediately
items.body: The OpenSSL helper that generates client certs writes its inputs and outputs to a per-request temp directory and removes the directory in a `finally` block, so a key written to disk for the duration of the signing call is gone before the HTTP response is returned.

releases.headline: PQC-only TLS enforcement via ClientHello inspection
releases.summary: A TCP-level gate inspects the raw TLS ClientHello and rejects connections that do not offer a PQC hybrid key-exchange group, before any classical handshake completes.

items.title: TCP-level PQC gate (`lib/pqc-gate.js`)
items.body: An outer `net.Server` parses the `supported_groups` extension from each incoming ClientHello. Connections that list at least one PQC hybrid group are piped to the internal HTTPS server; connections without one receive a TLS `handshake_failure` alert and are closed.

items.title: Localhost bypass for container health probes
items.body: Connections from `127.0.0.1` skip the gate so Docker `HEALTHCHECK` and other loopback probes can reach the HTTPS server without negotiating PQC.

items.title: `PQC_ENFORCE=false` opt-out
items.body: When the environment variable is set to `false`, the gate is bypassed entirely and PQC remains preferred but not required. This is intended as a transition aid while clients are upgraded; the default is enforcement on.

items.title: Gate fails closed on parse errors and timeouts
items.body: The ClientHello parser has a 5-second timeout and a 16 KB maximum buffer. Any parse error, timeout, or oversized record causes the connection to be closed before the handshake can proceed, so a malformed record cannot smuggle a classical session through the gate.

releases.headline: Resource-scoped sync tokens and stash sync integration
releases.summary: API keys can now be bound to a specific stash or bundle, and the admin UI gains a one-click flow for minting a sync token tied to a single stash.

items.title: Resource-scoped API keys (`boundStashId`, `boundBundleId`)
items.body: An API key may now be restricted to a single stash or a single bundle. Requests authenticated with a bound key are rejected if the target resource does not match the binding, limiting the blast radius of a leaked sync token.

items.title: Stash sync mode — persistent mutable bundle per stash
items.body: Enabling sync on a stash provisions a persistent mutable bundle that desktop sync clients use as their canonical sync target. The bundle survives client reconnects and is the anchor for the per-file sequence cursor.

items.title: Admin UI — sync toggle and one-click token generation
items.body: The stash admin page gains a sync toggle and a button that mints a sync-scoped, stash-bound API key in a single step, returning the token for the operator to copy into the desktop client.

releases.headline: Real-time file sync via WebSocket over PQC TLS
releases.summary: Adds a minimal RFC 6455 WebSocket channel at `GET /sync/ws` that streams file-change events to authenticated sync clients, with reconnection catch-up via a sequence cursor.

items.title: WebSocket sync channel at `GET /sync/ws`
items.body: Clients receive `file_added`, `file_replaced`, `file_removed`, and `heartbeat` events in real time. Authentication is enforced during the upgrade handshake; unauthorized upgrades are rejected before the socket opens.

items.title: Reconnect catch-up via sequence cursor
items.body: Clients reconnect with `?since=N` to replay every event after that sequence number, so a dropped connection cannot lose state. The cursor is the same per-file `seq` introduced in 1.3.0.

items.title: Conditional HTTPS with PQC hybrid key exchange
items.body: TLS now negotiates the `X25519MLKEM768` and `SecP256r1MLKEM768` hybrid groups when the client supports them, with classical fallback otherwise. The same TLS endpoint serves HTTP and WebSocket traffic.

items.title: Minimal RFC 6455 WebSocket implementation (`lib/ws.js`)
items.body: A dependency-free framer/parser supports the subset needed for the sync channel (text frames, ping/pong, close). Event dispatch inside the server uses an in-process `EventEmitter` so route handlers can publish without coupling to the socket layer.

items.title: `sync` API key scope
items.body: A new scope gates the WebSocket upgrade and the sync HTTP endpoints. Existing scopes are unchanged; operators must mint a new key (or extend an existing one) to enable sync access.

items.title: Certificate auto-reload for Let's Encrypt renewals
items.body: The HTTPS server reloads its certificate chain in place when the file on disk changes, so a renewal no longer requires a process restart that would drop every active WebSocket.

releases.headline: Mutable sync bundles — the foundation for file sync
releases.summary: Introduces a second bundle type that accepts post-creation file additions, replacements, and deletions, enabling sync-client workflows without altering the immutable snapshot path.

items.title: `bundleType` column with `snapshot` and `sync` modes
items.body: New bundles default to `snapshot` (immutable, existing behavior). Sync bundles accept file additions, replacements, and deletions after creation, with a per-file `seq` counter and `updatedAt` timestamp for change-feed ordering.

items.title: File replace and soft-delete for sync bundles
items.body: Re-uploading a file with the same `relativePath` overwrites the prior entry with a fresh encryption key. Deletions are soft (tombstone) and cleaned up after 30 days so reconnecting clients can observe the removal.

items.title: Rate-limited sync file delete endpoint
items.body: The new sync delete route is rate-limited to 100 requests per minute per token to bound the cost of a misbehaving or hostile client.

items.title: Snapshot bundles remain fully immutable
items.body: Existing snapshot bundles are unaffected by this release. The mutation paths are gated on `bundleType === 'sync'`, so the immutable contract for prior bundles holds with zero regression.
